diagrams.title: Elliptic Curve Visualization: y² = x³ + ax + b
diagrams.description: Visual representation of a sample elliptic curve showing its characteristic shape and symmetry
elements: Cartesian coordinate system with labeled x-axis and y-axis
Elliptic curve plotted as smooth, continuous, symmetric curve (banana-shaped)
Point at infinity (∞) marked at the top of the curve
Sample points P(x₁, y₁) and Q(x₂, y₂) shown on the curve
Horizontal dashed line showing symmetry about x-axis: if (x, y) is on curve, then (x, -y) is also on curve
Parameters a and b displayed with curve equation
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to visualize elliptic curves, understand their symmetry property, recognize the point at infinity, and see how the curve equation defines its shape. This foundation is essential for understanding point arithmetic.

diagrams.title: Elliptic Curve Point Addition: P + Q = R
diagrams.description: Geometric construction showing how two points on an elliptic curve are added to produce a third point
elements: Elliptic curve with two distinct points P and Q
Straight line drawn through P and Q intersecting the curve at a third point R'
Point R' reflected vertically across x-axis to get R = P + Q
For special case of point doubling (P + P): tangent line at P intersects curve at second point, then reflected to get 2P
Point at infinity (∞) serving as identity element: P + ∞ = P
Labels showing coordinates and modular arithmetic in finite field GF(p)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental point addition operation that forms the basis of all ECC computations. The geometric interpretation helps visualize why addition works, and the special cases (point at infinity, point doubling) are clearly shown.

diagrams.title: ECC Key Generation Process
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the complete workflow for generating ECC key pairs
elements: Block 1: Select Elliptic Curve - choose curve parameters (a, b) and prime p for GF(p)
Block 2: Select Base Point G - choose a generator point of large prime order n
Block 3: Generate Private Key - select random integer d where 1 ≤ d ≤ n-1
Block 4: Compute Public Key - calculate Q = d × G using point multiplication (repeated addition)
Arrow showing private key d kept secret
Arrow showing public key Q published with curve parameters (p, a, b, G, n)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the step-by-step key generation process and understand the mathematical relationship between private and public keys. The one-way nature of computing Q = d×G (easy) vs finding d given Q (hard) is emphasized.

diagrams.title: ECC Encryption/Decryption Flow (ECIES-style)
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the hybrid encryption process using ECC
elements: Sender side: Message m → Hash → Derive encryption key k₁
Generate random ephemeral key k
Compute k×G (ephemeral public key)
Compute shared secret S = k×Q (recipient's public key)
Derive symmetric keys (encryption, MAC) from S
Encrypt message using symmetric algorithm (AES)
Transmit: (k×G, ciphertext, MAC)
Recipient side: Use private key d to compute S = d×(k×G)
Decrypt and verify MAC
Output: Original message
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the hybrid approach combining ECC key exchange with symmetric encryption. This shows why ECC is practical for encrypting large messages while efficiently establishing shared secrets.

diagrams.title: ECDSA Digital Signature Process
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing message signing and verification using ECDSA
elements: Signing phase: Message m → Compute hash e = H(m)
Generate random k
Compute r = (k×G).x mod n
Compute s = k⁻¹(e + d×r) mod n
Output signature: (r, s)
Verification phase: Compute w = s⁻¹ mod n
Compute u₁ = e×w mod n, u₂ = r×w mod n
Compute point P = u₁×G + u₂×Q
Verify: Accept if r ≡ P.x mod n, else reject
Curve parameters (p, a, b, G, n) used throughout
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how ECDSA provides authentication and integrity. The mathematical steps show why verification works - it's computationally infeasible to forge signatures without the private key.

charts.title: ECC vs RSA vs DSA: Comprehensive Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Algorithm Type, Key Generation Complexity, Signature Speed, Encryption Speed, Security Level (bits), Typical Key Sizes, Best Use Cases. Rows: RSA, ECC (256-bit), DSA. Highlights: RSA 3072-bit ≈ ECC 256-bit security, ECC advantages in bandwidth and computation.

charts.title: Key Size Equivalence for Equal Security
charts.content: Table showing equivalent security levels: RSA key size | ECC key size | Symmetric key equivalent | Processing overhead ratio. Rows: 80-bit security (RSA 1024, ECC 160), 128-bit security (RSA 3072, ECC 256), 192-bit security (RSA 7680, ECC 384), 256-bit security (RSA 15360, ECC 521). Shows ECC achieves same security with 6-10x smaller keys.

charts.title: ECC Algorithm Selection Decision Tree
charts.content: Flowchart with decision points: Security requirement → Choose curve type (NIST, Brainpool, Curve25519) → Select key size based on application (160-bit for constrained IoT, 256-bit for general, 384-bit for high security) → Verify curve parameters (prime field, cofactor) → Select algorithm (ECDSA for signatures, ECIES for encryption, ECDH for key exchange).

charts.title: ECC Standards and Protocols Classification
charts.content: Classification chart grouping ECC implementations: Key Exchange (ECDH, ECDHE), Digital Signatures (ECDSA, EdDSA, Schnorr), Encryption (ECIES, ElGamal), Standards (ANSI X9.62, FIPS 186-4, NIST SP 800-56A/B), Applications (TLS/SSL, Bitcoin/Ethereum, Smart Cards, IoT/5G).

branches: Mathematical Foundations: Elliptic curves over finite fields (GF(p), GF(2^m)), Point addition formulas, Group theory and cyclic subgroups, Order of a point, Base point G selection
Key Operations: Point addition (P + Q), Point multiplication (k × P), Discrete logarithm problem (find k given P and k×P), One-way function property
Key Generation: Select curve parameters (a, b, p), Choose base point G of order n, Generate random private key d, Compute public key Q = d×G
Encryption & Key Exchange: ECDH for key agreement, ECIES hybrid encryption, ElGamal-style encryption, ECDHE in TLS handshake
Digital Signatures: ECDSA algorithm, Signature generation (r, s), Signature verification process, EdDSA (Edwards curves)
Security Considerations: Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP), Side-channel attacks, Curve selection (NIST vs Brainpool), Backdoor concerns, Post-quantum considerations
Advantages over RSA: 10x smaller key sizes, Faster computations, Lower bandwidth, Equal or higher security, Efficient hardware implementation
Practical Applications: Secure web browsing (TLS), Cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum), Smart cards and HSMs, IoT and constrained devices, Mobile devices, Government and military communications
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive mind map showing relationships between mathematical foundations, cryptographic operations, algorithms, security considerations, and real-world applications. Helps students connect theory to practice and understand why ECC is preferred in modern cryptographic systems.
